cataloging_subject.label: Piers the Plowman's Crede
cataloging_subject.scope_note: One anonymous late-fourteenth-century alliterative poem. The XML's second DIV1, headed CREDO., is the intrinsic concluding section in which Piers answers the narrator's request; it is not a separately cataloged work.

identifiers.scheme: University of Michigan Digital Collections item ID

notes: Modern discovery title. The corpus source and Skeat use the historical spelling 'Pierce the Ploughmans Crede'.

scope.description: Heading of the first XML DIV1.

titles.value: Cros and Curteis Crist this bygynnynge spede
notes: Discovery form normalized only enough to replace thorn with 'th'; witness and edition spellings vary.

agents.name: Walter W. Skeat
notes: Editor of the 1867 EETS edition represented by the digital source; not the medieval author.

assertion.notes: No securely identified medieval author is known.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1867 EETS source edition, not medieval authorship.

assertion.notes: Dean uses Walter Brut's 1393 trial as the terminus a quo and places composition before 1401.

notes: The sixteenth-century dates of the complete surviving manuscripts are witness dates, not composition dates.

places.label: Southwest Midlands
notes: Used as a cautious regional normalization of Dean's statement that the author probably came from the Southwest Midland region.

assertion.notes: The source supports probable authorial origin, not a complete dialectological localization independent of the late witnesses.

notes: Dean says the probable Southwest Midland author had London connections; this is not a claim that the poem was composed in London.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin liturgical words and formulas are embedded vocabulary, not a second work-level language.

form_statements.description: Unrhymed alliterative long-line verse. The terminal AMEN. is encoded as a closer rather than a numbered verse line.

subject_statements.label: The Apostles' Creed and lay religious instruction

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English alliterative satire in which a narrator seeking to learn the Apostles' Creed consults representatives of the four mendicant orders. Their rivalry, wealth, and demands for money expose their failure as spiritual teachers. The poor plowman Piers instead explains true Christian belief and recites the Creed, joining antifraternal polemic to a Wycliffite ideal of apostolic poverty and plain faith.

The first XML DIV1 contains lines 1-794; after the narrator asks Piers to teach him, the second DIV1 headed CREDO. continues the same poem. It contains printed verse lines 795-855 and the terminal closer AMEN. The division is retained as a useful locator but does not create a second work.

The work has 855 verse lines in Skeat's printed lineation and DIMEV. The XML has 856 L elements because the page-32 running head GOD AMEND ALL FALSE FRIARS ! was mistakenly encoded as L N=847. XML L labels 848-856 therefore correspond to printed lines 847-855. The final AMEN. is preserved separately as CLOSER and is not a verse line. Dean's later METS edition numbers 850 lines, an edition-specific lineation retained as a discrepancy rather than substituted for the source edition's extent.

extent.description: One verse work in two XML DIV1 sections: 794 verse lines before the CREDO. heading and 61 verse lines in the intrinsic Creed-and-conclusion section. Raw encoding metrics are 856 L elements plus one CLOSER: 855 L elements represent verse, one L element is a printed running head, and the CLOSER preserves AMEN. The eight supplied DIV2 line-range groups are navigation, not intellectual verse groups; the group labeled Lines 101 through 200 actually includes XML L 101-201.

content_parts.label: The narrator's search and Piers's antifraternal speech
content_parts.summary: The narrator seeks instruction from the four orders of friars, encounters Piers, and hears his extended critique of mendicant wealth and hypocrisy before asking to be taught the Creed.
notes: The eight nested DIV2 elements are supplied hundred-line navigation groups; one has an incorrect displayed range and none is treated as an authored subdivision.

content_parts.label: CREDO. and conclusion
content_parts.summary: Piers teaches the narrator the Creed and the poem closes with doctrinal reflection, an authorial correction formula, and a prayer that faithful friars be saved and false friars amended.

locators.value: DIV1.2; L 795-846 and 848-856; CLOSER AMEN. (L 847 is a non-verse running head)

notes: The XML heading marks an intrinsic rhetorical transition, not a second independent work.

related_works.title: Piers Plowman
notes: Dean identifies Langland's poem as a literary influence and the source of the Piers figure; this does not make the Crede a section of Piers Plowman.

evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY; DIV1.1-2; all DIV2, HEAD, L, and CLOSER elements
evidence.summary: The exact repository source identifies PPlCreed, the May 1996 U-M/OTA encoding, Skeat's 1867 source edition, the encoded selection and editorial declarations, and the complete two-DIV1 text structure.

evidence.citation: Structural and lineation audit of PPlCreed.xml against Skeat's 1867 scan, performed 2026-07-15.
evidence.summary: The audit found 856 consecutive L labels, but established from the printed pages that XML L 847 is a running head rather than verse. It maps XML L 848-856 to printed lines 847-855, confirms the terminal CLOSER, and identifies the supplied Lines 101 through 200 group as actually containing L 101-201.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, PPlCreed, Pierce the Ploughmans Crede [electronic edition].
evidence.summary: The exact item page confirms the identifier, title, Skeat editor statement, 1867 EETS source, OTA number, public full text, two-part table of contents, and current public-domain / No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., Pierce the Ploughmans Crede (about 1394 A.D.), EETS Original Series 30 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1867), Internet Archive scan.
evidence.locator: PDF pages 41-72, printed pages 1-32; especially PDF pages 71-72, printed pages 31-32
evidence.summary: The edition supplies the complete printed source text, title and editorial statement, base-witness and collation declarations, editorial method, 855-line printed lineation, page-32 running head, and terminal AMEN.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, James Catalogue of Western Manuscripts, R.3.15, James 595.
evidence.locator: Main Details; Contents III; IIIF manifest canvases 629-651
evidence.summary: The official catalog identifies the paper codex, manuscript dates and hands, Piers Plowman's Crede as the final twelve-leaf item, its incipit and explicit, Skeat's use of it, and the complete public IIIF object. Direct image inspection maps the work to fols. 317r-328r.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 2.0-alpha.1, record 1086, Pierce the Ploughmans Crede.
evidence.summary: Versioned evidence for the title, normalized incipit, 855 alliterative lines, NIMEV 663, MED BIB2823, two complete manuscript witnesses, Harley fragment, and 1553 print witness. The Royal witness's copied 'Lines 172-207 only' source note conflicts with its full incipit, explicit, folio span, and official catalog and is treated as a data defect.

evidence.citation: James M. Dean, ed., Introduction to Piers the Plowman's Crede and text, in Six Ecclesiastical Satires (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1991), Middle English Texts Series digital edition.
evidence.locator: Piers the Plowman's Crede introduction, text, notes, and bibliography; downloadable PDF pages 2-50
evidence.summary: Current scholarly evidence for anonymous authorship, 1393-1401 composition, probable Southwest Midland origin and London connections, alliterative antifraternal satire, Wycliffite influence, plot and intrinsic Creed conclusion, witness census, later editorial sources, and Dean's separate 850-line editorial lineation.

open_questions.question: How narrowly can the poem's authorial dialect be localized independently of the sixteenth-century witness spellings?
next_steps: Consult a dialectological study or Linguistic Atlas profile specific to the Crede.
Distinguish authorial localization from the copying dialects of Trinity R.3.15, Royal 18 B XVII, Harley 78, and the 1553 print.
notes: Southwest Midlands is retained as a tentative cataloging region because Dean phrases it as probable authorial origin rather than a full linguistic demonstration.

open_questions.question: Which editorial joining or splitting decisions account for Dean's 850-line text versus Skeat and DIMEV's 855-line count?
next_steps: Collate Dean's line numbering against Skeat's printed lines 1-855 and record every joined or split long line.
Keep source-specific line locators explicit rather than treating either numbering system as universal.

notes: The work is anonymous. Skeat's title-page association with Langland's Piers Plowman and older catalog attributions must not be converted into authorship.
The first-quarter-sixteenth-century Royal manuscript and the possibly later Trinity hand are transmission evidence, not substitutes for the 1393-1401 composition date.
The running head encoded as XML L 847 is excluded from every intellectual verse-line metric.
The current OTA, U-M, MEDictionary, repository, scan, and manuscript-image rights statements apply to different layers and are recorded only in the linked lineage manifest.
